title: Chapter 5: The Stranger on the Doorstep
content: I shift from the apartment this time.

Murray Hill. One AM. The city humming through the walls, Mika's voice still ringing in my ears — *You can't live in three places at once, Ave* — and I can't answer her because she's right and I hate that she's right and the only thing I know how to do is leave.

My thumb finds the Fork app before my brain catches up. The green path pulses at the edge of the screen like a heartbeat. Clonakilty. I don't know what that word means. I don't know anything about the green path except that it's the one I haven't taken.
I press it.
The world folds.

It doesn't fold gently. It folds like a letter crammed into an envelope — creased, hurried, indifferent to the contents. My stomach drops, my vision smears, and there's a sound like the air being sucked out of a room. Then—
Rain. On glass.

I'm standing behind a wooden counter, my hands flat on its surface, breathing hard. The scent hits me first: fresh bread, warm and yeasty, layered under something animal and soft. Cat fur. The air tastes like rain and brown sugar.
Three cats are watching me.

A fat orange tabby sits on the register, his eyes half-lidded, judging my arrival with the particular disdain only an orange cat can muster. A black-and-white tuxedo perches on a bookshelf near the window, tail flicking. A lean gray one sits by the door, ears pricked, head tilted like he's trying to remember if he knows me.
I look down at my hands. Flour under the nails. A faded apron tied around my waist. A small scar on my left knuckle I don't recognize.

The café is small — maybe ten tables, mismatched chairs, a chalkboard menu above the counter written in handwriting that is and isn't mine. Sweeping loops, a heart over the 'i' in 'Scones.' I have never once drawn a heart over an 'i' in my life.
Paws & Pause, the sign above the door reads.
I laugh. It comes out strangled and wet.
It's perfect. It's terrifying.
I don't remember walking upstairs, but here I am.

The flat above the café is tiny — one room, barely enough space to breathe. A bed shoved against the wall under a window. Hand-knitted blankets in muted greens and creams, the stitches uneven in a way that suggests this Avery taught herself to knit alone, at night, with only the sound of the sea for company. A shelf of poetry books — Mary Oliver, Seamus Heaney, Ocean Vuong — dog-eared and spine-cracked and loved. A small kitchen with a single mug in the drying rack. A succulent on the windowsill, somehow still alive.
This version of me lives alone. This version of me chose alone.

I walk to the window and the Atlantic opens up in front of me — churning grey, endless, indifferent. The sky hangs low and dark, and the rain comes down in silver threads, and the wind shakes the glass in its frame. The sea doesn't care about me. It doesn't care about my résumé or my timelines or the three lives I'm failing to live simultaneously. It's just there, vast and unbothered, and something in my chest cracks open at the sight of it.
"So this is what giving up looks like," I whisper.
But it doesn't feel like giving up.
It feels like breathing for the first time.
I press my palm flat against the cold glass. The sea stares back. I let it.
The hours pass strangely.

I find my rhythm in this body — this version of my body that knows where the espresso machine's sweet spot is, that reaches for the rag on the hook without looking, that moves between tables with the ease of muscle memory borrowed from a stranger. I make coffee for afternoon regulars whose names I should know but don't. I smile and nod and the words come out of my mouth like they were always meant to be there. The orange tabby — Toast, I somehow know — winds between my ankles while I work. The tuxedo — Socks — naps on a stack of newspapers. The gray one — Fog — sits at the door like he's waiting for someone.
Eleven PM. I'm closing up.

I wipe down the last table, the rag moving in slow circles, the quiet settling around me like the hand-knitted blankets upstairs. The rain has gotten heavier — I can hear it drumming against the windows, a steady percussion that almost drowns out the tick of the old clock above the door. I scoop kibble into three bowls, and Toast headbutts my shin in appreciation, and Socks stretches and yawns, and Fog watches from his post by the door, ears rotating like satellite dishes.
Then I hear it.
A thud. Outside. Heavy and wrong.
I freeze, rag still in hand.
Silence. Rain. Wind.
Another sound — a groan, low and guttural, the kind of sound a person makes when they're trying very hard not to make any sound at all.
My pulse kicks into something feral. The smart thing to do is call the gardaí. The smart thing to do is stay inside, lock the door tighter, wait. The smart thing to do is not walk toward the sound.
I drop the rag.
I walk toward the door.
Fog doesn't move from his spot. He looks up at me with pale green eyes, and I swear to God he nods, and then I'm unlocking the door and pulling it open and the rain hits my face like a slap and—
He's leaning against the doorframe.

Left shoulder soaked in something darker than rain. Leather jacket — black, ruined, torn open at the seam where something went through it and came out the other side. Face pale as the grey sky behind him. Jaw set like he's holding himself together through sheer, furious willpower.
Gray eyes.
Those SAME gray eyes.
Finding mine through the rain.

My breath stops. The world narrows to the space between us — rain and blood and recognition I can't explain thrumming through my veins like an electric current. I've seen those eyes twice now. In a penthouse kitchen. Across a lecture hall. They belong to a man I've never spoken to in timelines where I didn't save him and couldn't reach him and—
"Don't scream."
His voice is Irish, rough as road grit, barely more than a breath. He says it like he's been practicing the words all the way here.
"Please."
He collapses.

I catch him — or maybe I don't, maybe he's just too heavy and we both go down, my knees hitting the wet doorstep, his weight against my chest, cold rain soaking through my shirt. He's heavier than he looks. His leather jacket is slick and freezing under my hands, and the smell of it hits me — wet leather and iron, the metallic tang of fresh blood sharp enough to cut through the rain.
I drag him inside.

I don't think about it. I don't ask his name or check for weapons or wonder if I'm making the worst decision of any of my lives. My hands grip the collar of his ruined jacket, my heels dig into the floor, and I pull. He leaves a wet trail across the café — rainwater and something redder — and Toast jumps off the counter with an indignant yowl as I haul the stranger past.
I lock the door. My hands are shaking. I lock it anyway.

The first-aid kit is under the counter behind the espresso machine. I find it by instinct — this Avery's instinct, the one who apparently keeps gauze and antiseptic and surgical thread and curved needles in a kit that would make a field medic weep with pride. I don't question it. I don't have time.

I cut his jacket off. Scissors from the kit, sawing through wet leather, and the sound of it is obscene in the quiet café. Underneath, his shirt is black with blood — not all of it wet, some of it drying, which means he's been bleeding for a while. Which means he walked here, or ran here, or crawled here with a wound that should have dropped him blocks ago.

The entry wound is clean. Through-and-through, just below the left clavicle. Gunshot. I know it's a gunshot the way I know where the espresso machine's sweet spot is — this body knows, even if I don't.

I clean it first. Antiseptic on gauze, and the smell of it burns my nose, sharp and chemical. His jaw tightens but he doesn't move, doesn't make a sound, doesn't even flinch. The muscles in his neck stand out like cables. His breathing is shallow and controlled — military breathing, I realize. Someone taught him how to breathe through pain.
Toast jumps onto the counter and settles in, watching the procedure with the focus of a surgical supervisor. His orange tail flicks once. Twice.
I thread the needle with hands that won't stop shaking. I take a breath. I press the needle into his skin.

It slides through easier than I expect — flesh parting for the curve of it, the thread following, pulling the wound closed. I tie the first suture. Then the second. Then the third. My fingers are slick with blood and my heartbeat is loud enough to hear over the rain and I am stitching a stranger's gunshot wound in a cat café in a small Irish town at midnight and none of this is real except all of it is.
He makes one sound.
Just one — a sharp intake of breath, hissing between his teeth, when I pull the thread tight on the fourth suture. His left hand — cold, wet, trembling — lifts from the floor and finds mine.
Not grabbing. Not pulling.
Holding.
Like an anchor.

His fingers are freezing against my wrist, his grip weak but deliberate, and I understand — not through words but through the pressure of his hand — that he's not holding on to me. He's holding on to something. Anything. The fact that it's me is incidental.
I don't let go.

I tie the fifth suture one-handed, my left hand pinned by his. Toast tilts his head. The rain keeps falling. Socks has emerged from his newspaper nest and is sitting two feet away, watching with unblinking yellow eyes. Fog hasn't moved from the door.

I bandage the wound. Gauze and medical tape, wrapped tight enough to put pressure on the site. His skin is cold under mine — too cold, shock-cold — and I grab one of the hand-knitted blankets from upstairs and drape it over him before I can think about what I'm doing. Then I sit on the floor next to him, my back against the counter, his hand still wrapped around mine.
The café is dark except for the glow of the streetlamp outside, painting amber shapes on the ceiling. The rain speaks against the windows. Toast purrs from the counter.
I wait.
He wakes without warning.

One moment his breathing is slow and even — the deep, deliberate rhythm of someone who's trained themselves to sleep light and wake faster. The next, his gray eyes are open, sharp, scanning the room with the quick, assessment-heavy sweep of someone cataloging exits and threats.
His gaze finds me.
The sharpness softens. Just a fraction. Just enough.

Three cats have arranged themselves around him while he slept. Toast is on his chest, a warm orange weight rising and falling with his breathing. Socks is curled against his right side, pressed into the curve of his body. Fog is lying across his boots like a sentinel.
He blinks at them. Something flickers across his face — confusion, maybe, or the ghost of a feeling he doesn't have a name for.
"You're not afraid of me," he says.

Not a question. An observation. His voice is rougher now, scraped raw, the Irish accent thicker around the edges. He says it like he's filing it away. Like my lack of fear is a data point he needs to remember.

I should be afraid. There's a bleeding stranger on my café floor with a gunshot wound and military breathing and gray eyes I've seen in timelines I haven't lived yet, and I don't know his name, and I don't know why he came here, and I don't know why he chose *this doorstep*.
But his hand is still in mine. Cold and steady. And my hand hasn't let go.
"I haven't decided yet," I say.
He looks at me for a long moment. The rain fills the silence between us. Then — so small I almost miss it — the corner of his mouth lifts.

It's not a smile. It's the wreckage of one. A smile that's been broken and rebuilt so many times it's barely holding together. But it's real. I can tell because it reaches his eyes, just for a second, softening the gray into something almost warm.
Then his eyes close again. His grip on my hand doesn't loosen.

I sit on the floor of Paws & Pause, the Atlantic howling outside, rain on the windows, cats on guard, and a stranger's blood drying on my shirt. I don't know his name. I don't know why he's bleeding. I don't know what timeline I'm living in or what version of myself I'm becoming or what any of this means.
But I know his face.
And I know I'm staying.
